speaker: interviewer
text: This is roll three with Martin Kohn. [INAUDIBLE] be able to get the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which camp was it? What camp was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, Plaszow. Is that-- that was

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we plastered. And then they gave us a sort of an emblem with an M-- you know, that we're [GERMAN], you know, which--

speaker: interviewer
text: Meaning plasterers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Meaning plasterers, you know, or, or, construction-- you know, in construction. And of course, you know, they-- I mean, you felt the-- we weren't necessarily-- you know, I mean, it didn't get to us directly. Except, you know, like for instance, by this time, you know, where it was, you know, towards the later-- you know, maybe a month or so before they took us away from Plaszow. You know, and there were these killings going on. You know, like for instance, they were taking people up to a, to the hill. You know? And of course, you know, these people never came back, you know? But for some reason or other, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we didn't-- I mean, we were too young, maybe, to be as, you know, to realize. Because like I said, you know, the older people who knew probably more, understood more, did not relate to us, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your brother have to say? What did he think about it all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't kind of-- you know, we knew what's happening. And of course, you know, we knew enough You understand? That the war, you understand, is gonna come to a quick-- you know, that's the understanding we left with.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, we were hoping, you know, that things really, you know, will end soon, you know? And then, of course, you know, we realized, you know, that things don't end, you know, like I said. And then, one day they took us, you know, away from Plaszow. And they took us to Gross-Rosen. Before you get to Gross-Rosen--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --here you had this guy shooting from the balcony.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had to walk underneath.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And then of course--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you knew a lot about the killings.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that for the two of you? Did you ever think that you were next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's not only the-- well, you never knew who was next. You never knew who was next. You know, what, what--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you  know anybody who got killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't. There was-- you see, who they were after you could never tell. But I mean, there was people who tried to run away, you understand, or break out. And they were caught, you know, and they brought them back and killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the camp, they even had, you know, gentile-- you know, young kids, you understand, who were-- sort of did some kind of mischief. And they gave them red blotches on their backs. You know? And of course, you know-- but there was a few hangings. Did you see any?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Many hangings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I, I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they, they would-- of course, you know, they would-- everybody was called out to the Appellplatz, you understand? And you know, they made speeches, whatever. And you know, you were standing. And you realized, you know, that they would take and they will tell you why. And if somebody else will dare to commit something similar, you know. And they hung them, you know? And in a sense, you know, we were very maybe oblivious to things as long as it didn't happen to you, or something. I mean, it wasn't something that-- we knew that there isn't-- I mean, the, the, the loss of us, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And don't forget, I mean, the Jews knew, this is sort of a lot that they've been going through. And of course, we didn't realize, you know, to that particular-- you know-- in that sense, I mean. We knew that killing's been going on, and massacres, you know? I mean, especially if you were a, you know, Orthodox kid, you know? I mean, the massacres and, you know, what happened in the temples, you know, in Israel and everywhere, the Spanish Inquisition-- I mean, you knew these things about it. And in that particular sense, I think we felt quite a great loss, you know, as far as-- that we are, you understand, sort of surrounded by, by enemies. You know? Did you spend any time thinking about missing your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We thought about them all the time. All the time. You know, what happened, and what-- how are they faring, and, you know, the kids, and everything like that, you know? And of course, we had our nightmares, you know, and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you still have nightmares?

speaker: interviewee
text: Occasionally. Occasionally.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you dream about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I might be dreaming of being, you know, chased, like, or something. Or being persecuted, or something like that. I mean-- and of course, you know, like, like I say, I always try to put things like that out of my mind. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: What things do you work hardest at putting out of your mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- well, first of all, you know, that you survived. [CRYING] And then they didn't. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, you know, like my brother, you know-- we were in the same bed together. You know, and two weeks later, you know, just for sheer negligence-- you know, nobody-- nobody even looked at us. You know? And not even gave us-- if we weren't able to, to help ourselves, you know, just give one another a little water or maybe just take a little towel, or whatever we had, to put on somebody's head that they shouldn't burn up from fever. And this was after, you know-- of course, a lot of people, I suppose, didn't want to catch the disease. You know, it was a typhoid, you know? And-- So that's what you go back to the most?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Of course, you know-- you know, after, you know, I was for seven years alone, after the war, until I got married. You know, and once I got married, and had a family, I mean, things came a little easier. You know, it came easier. But also, you know, there was many, many days I was just not feeling well, not knowing-- you know, being depressed. You know? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And you know, that's, that's mainly-- and of course, you know, from time to time, you know, you watched certain things which remind you. And you kind of carry it. And of course, also I think we have a lot of anger also. And, well, you know, you're angry at the Hungarians. And you don't necessarily pick one person out and say, I'm angry at him because he's Hungarian, or I'm angry at him because he's German. But in general, you understand, I am. I feel a lot of anger towards them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, you know, like for instance these particular camps, you know, like Auschwitz, we didn't-- we weren't sort of forced into, into labor. You understand? I mean, it was in the summertime. And the hunger started just, just before. You know? And then they took us to Gross-Rosen. And they started giving you a portion of this, and a portion of that. And you realized, you know, as soon as you ate, you were hungry before and you were hungry-- you know. I mean-- and then later on, of course, they didn't give you any, any substantial food at all. You know? So there you were constantly just starving.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, they took us to Gross-Rosen. And of course, they made a-- they, they re-- you know, they take you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Rerooted you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, first you come over there. They made a selection of certain people, you understand, that they're going to send them here or there. And they took you. And of course, there was a-- later on, I found out that there was also over there a, you know-- what do you call it, where they got rid of people? I think they had a crematorium in Gross-Rosen also. But at that particular time, they killed so many people, that they-- which I figured out just, you know, afterwards-- that they needed people. They need to have bodies to send to these different camps where they made commitments to send so many people to work. So that's why they, they sometimes, you know, saved, you know, younger children when they know they didn't have no actually labor value.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we stayed a couple of days in Gross-Rosen. And they sent us to Bolkenhain. And this Bolkenhain-- there was this lageralteste. His name was Wolf. And actually, he was sent away from another camp because he was too cruel. I don't know how they got rid of him. But this-- he was near Dornhau. And he came, you know, and he was a sadistic-- I mean, he was extremely sadistic, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: This camp, we came in originally, I think, maybe 600, 700 people. There was a couple of thousand people who perished in that particular camp, in this little camp, in a period of about-- from, I think from, let's say, August, end of August, till February.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, for the first couple of weeks, we were-- they didn't know what to do with us, you know? You know, some-- even though they were young, there was a factory over there, a munition factory, you know, that they worked in. And, you know, people-- I remember after a while, you know, the oil in their clothing, you know, they couldn't wash it and couldn't do anything. And they were-- it gave them a miserable existence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because, you know, they-- just the oil. I mean, they could never get clean enough-- you know, wash, you know? And it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it enter their skin, or something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I suppose so, you know? But anyway, so-- but for a while, they made us work in the kitchen, you know, like peeling potatoes and carrots. And of course, you know, eating too much. While you're there, and you were eating, of course, you had to watch yourself so nobody sees. You know? Otherwise, they would punish you, or something, or hit you, you know? And of course, you know, I was. For different things I was hit many times. They had these-- from raw skin, or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Belts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they belts? What did they hit you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they're not a belt. You know, they're, they're like those--

speaker: interviewer
text: Whips?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whips. Yeah, whips. You know, maybe a meter and a half. You know, they would hit you. If he was merciful, he would hit you on your behind. If not, then he hit you on the back, or wherever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were they, Germans? Who hit you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not necessarily. There was the, the lageralteste, who was a German. He was a, I think a criminal. He was a criminal prisoner. He probably committed murder, or something like that. And he was made the, you know, the lageralteste. You know, he was over everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, of course, you know, there was kapos, who were different work details, you know? And then there was the blockalteste, who took care of each particular barrack. You know, he was the overseer. I mean, he would collect the food. He would, you know, divide it, and so on. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, I remember at one particular time in this particular-- and you know, this was already the wintertime. And I mean, after a while, after we started-- you know, stopped working in the kitchen, and they were sending us out, you know, to dig these tunnels-- because it was right next to the factory. Supposedly, they were supposed to move in the factory, you know, and, you know, to-- from bombing. In case they would bomb the factory, they will move it into the tunnels. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this in Bolkenhain?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Bolkenhain. And then, day by day-- you know, you had to get up about 6 o'clock in the morning. I think it was 6:00. Maybe earlier. You know, and then everybody had to stay on the Appellplatz. And this was in the middle of the winter. And we didn't have no clothing, you know? I mean, what we had is a suit. And later on they gave us, like, an overcoat, but a very thin, you know, cotton type of-- you know, no under-- I don't think we had underwear at all. And you know, the wind, you know, the wind chill factor was so-- you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I remember, actually, this was-- just going back. You know, we arrived in Bolkenhain. It must have been, like, in August. So it was-- it must have been early holidays, you know? And I remember we were trying to pray. You know, people were trying, you know, to pray. It was Rosh Hashanah. And that only time we really broke down, you know, and cried. You know, realizing, you know, where we are and what's happening to us, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, of course, another incident happened. You know, they gave us-- they came from-- I don't know if it was the Red Cross, or it was from Switzerland-- some delegation. And they came to visit the camp. And of course, every time-- I don't remember if it was once or twice, they came to visit. And they gave us some kind of present. I don't remember as far as food. But they gave us a pair of socks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we wore the socks. And we didn't have no shoes, you know, just these wooden shoes, you know, with just the front, you know, to stick in, you know? And of course, people, you know, wore it out. You know? And they made inspections. They were supposed to make inspections. So I knew how to use a needle and thread, you know? So I tried to fix some people. And I fixed everybody's socks, except mine. They found a hole. So for that, they gave me five or something, you know? Five?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five whips. Five, or maybe more. But for some reason or other, I was able to take that particular one.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, of course, you know, in the wintertime we used to go out to work, you know? And we would find pieces of wood, you know? We'd bring it in. Because otherwise there was no, no way of heating our little-- you know, they had, like, a, a stove. But the wood was frozen and everything. How do you start a fire? And nobody dared, you know? The slats that we had in the beds, you know, was already so that we couldn't take any more to make, to start a fire. So I says, you know what? I'm gonna go into the latrine, which is the washroom. And I'll take a couple of briquettes-- you know, coal. And maybe we'll be able to start a fire. So one kapo, you know, happened to see me. So he dragged me over to-- he dragged me to the lager-- you know-- alteste. You know? And you know-- for punishment.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I knew something, you know, not good would happen from this. So in the meantime, the lageralteste saw the lagerfuhrer, the German, coming in. So he went to greet him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I says-- you know, so I thought to myself. I says, this is the end. If he catch-- I mean, if he comes over, right away, a bullet, you know? That's it. So I just snuck away, you know? And they were looking and looking and looking. And you know, I suppose for some reason or other-- or the lageralteste didn't report it to the lagerfuhrer, you understand? And for some reason or other, it was-- you know, so I was lucky in that particular respect. And of course, you know, then in February, they started, you know, the Russians started coming closer. And they-- we were evacuated. And we were marched. We were marched, force marched, you know, to Hirschberg, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was the rainy season. And snow and sleet and all kind of-- you know, and they were, you know, marching us. And people who couldn't-- you know, they were just shooting people on the side, you know, like-- you know. And of course, then one particular place we stopped, there were-- you know, they put this into a barn for-- you know. And there was a lot of people who tried to run away, or they had an excuse that they were, you know, trying to run away. And they were shooting right and left. Because he was a very sadistic-- you know, this lagerfuhrer was very sadistic.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you know, on the march, it was like for a a free-for-all. You know? There wasn't-- if you just stepped out or you couldn't get along, they would drag you out and, and just shoot you. You know? So anyway-- Stay with the march. Tell me more about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, we were marching, marching towards this particular-- well, we didn't know our destination. We didn't know where they are taking us. But after a while-- I suppose this particular camp was a larger camp. You know, there was the camp itself. It was a big camp. And then there must have been, from other camps, you know, they brought people in, you know, to evacuation.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the camp whose name you do not remember, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was near Dornhau?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or it was near--

speaker: interviewee
text: Near, near Hirschberg. There was-- near Hirschberg, there was a camp. And they called it a [? kranken's ?] camp. And for some reason or other, which God only knows, they took so many people. I don't think it was more than about maybe 50 people, for some reason or other. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was after the march?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no. We came into this particular camp, Hirschberg. In this particular Hirschberg, there was evacuations going on from everywhere, which is, you know, camps from all over. And they brought the people in. And then from there, they evacuated them again, marching further. Now, they, they said-- you know, they called out that anybody who doesn't feel, you know, that he could walk any more should-- you know. So we were resigned, me and my brother. Because my brother was much weaker. And I was resigned. I didn't-- I says, whatever happens. We didn't necessarily know if they're going to take us any place, or-- I says, whatever happens happens. And they took us for a small-- oh, I think a few kilometers. They walked us over to this camp. And this camp, for some reason or other, there was Wehrmacht only. There was no SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Wehrmacht are the soldiers of the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the regular army. The regular army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they as vicious as the others?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they weren't vicious at all. I mean, they just let us stay in the camp. Of course, food wasn't really, you know, anything. But they gave you a slice of bread and a, and a little soup, and that's about it. But he was a, like an elderly Weimar, from the Weimar Republic, I think. You know, he looked, at that time, in his 70s.

speaker: interviewee
text: And just the only thing-- in the morning, they would call us out to stand in line, you know? And they counted us over-- you know, the usual routine. And then we went back, you know? But that was for a couple of weeks. And-- You didn't work there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't work. Except, you know, towards the end, they would, they would take us out, walk us-- I don't know, a certain distance, maybe a couple of kilometers, you know, to dig these ditches. They call it [GERMAN], you know, which was--

speaker: interviewer
text: I just want to get back to the march.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And ask you, how long? How many days? And what did you do at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. At night, they might-- you know, they would push you to the side, you know, on the road. You understand? First of all, there was a few nights we keeped on-- we kept on walking. We kept walking and walking, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: They did give us just very little provision, you know, each day. I think maybe they skipped a day or so. But they would give us, you know, like a bread, you know, black bread. You know, like-- what do you call it? Those-- Loaves?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a small, small loaf of bread, you know? And they would cut it maybe in 10 pieces. Sometimes they would give you, like, a piece of margarine with it. Sometimes not. Or, they would give you a little of-- they called-- what they called marmalade. You know? And that's about it. And water, of course, I don't even recall, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many do you think started the march?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, from our camp, I suppose it was 700, around like that. You know, because like I mentioned, you know, that people died away. Constantly died away from-- you know, there was elderly people. I mean, elderly people. You could imagine a man of 40, 40, 45, you understand, who hasn't eaten-- you know, I mean, they, they needed food more than even us children. And they worked and marched and everything. You know, they needed more food.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they, they, they-- day by day, you know, they dropped weight. And they became like, like, what we called Muselmann-- you know, like for instance, starved people. You know, like skin and bone.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, and they, they just fell away by the wayside. You know, every, every day, there were people taken away, you know, died. I don't know. Sometimes, you know, they had a, like a-- I don't know. They didn't call it a hospital, but a place, you know, where there was an office over there. And they might have-- we had a suspicion, you know, that they might have given them, you know, an injection, and away-- put them away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So 700 people started approximately.

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately 600, 700 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many people finished the march?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you can't-- really, I can't estimate, like I said, because then when we came into the camp, there was other people. I'm sure there must have been about 100, 150 people, you know, killed on the road, you know? And that depends. You know, I mean, the Germans, depending, you know, what towns they were going by and where they could do the killing. And I'm sure that they must have-- right away, somebody must have come, you know, and pick up these poor people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the people in the small towns know what was going on when you were marching through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, certainly they knew-- I mean, like for instance when I was in Bolkenhain, in this particular camp, you know, every day that we marched to the, to the factories and to the workplace, you know, there was population. It was German population. You know, they must have, must have seen. They must have, you know-- I mean, the camps were open. You know? And, let's say, maybe 500 or 600 yards away, there were people lived, you know, in homes and things like that. So they--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're saying they must have known?

speaker: interviewee
text: They must have known. You know? They must have known. And then of course, you know, like for instance, right next to our camp, there was-- outside, there were other camps, which there was Russian, Polish soldiers. You know, soldiers and civilians-- you know, women and things like that. And they, they, they've seen, you know, what was going on. You know? But--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in this kranken--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In the sick--

speaker: interviewer
text: --which means sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sick, sick camp. In other words, people who were sick, or they were weak, were taken over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they didn't have a crematorium there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was a very small camp. I don't think there was more than 250 people, maybe not even that much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then at the end, you got to the last camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Then they took us by train. You know, because these other people who were in Hirschberg, you know, at that particular time-- which I found out later-- and their, their lot was really, really, you know-- traveling, you know, in these-- even in-- first of all, they marched them in these weathers where, at that particular time between February and March, there is rains, and sleet, and all kind of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, freezing.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was freezing weather. At night you used to freeze. You know, you would freeze your bone. You know? And in the daytime, they, you know, were marching, you know? And then, of course, at certain spots they, they would take them and put them into these wagons. Some people, they tried to-- this was already towards the end of the war, you know? I mean, the war was going on all around them. Some, they were trying to take up to the mountains and, you know, get rid of them. And lots of times, they were cut off. You know, the trains were cut off, so they had to take them back. And they wound up in [? Buchenwald. ?] So Martin, what were you most angry at the Germans about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were angry for the inhumanity-- the way they treated human beings, you know? I mean, first of all, you know, just going and uproot you from your place. I mean, of course, figuring, what right do they have? I mean, they are Germans. They have their country.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand war, you understand? OK? But I mean, this was a country which they were supposed to be allies. You know, and they came in, you understand, they uprooted, and, and took your family, and then killed them for only being Jewish, you know? And of course, you know, Jewish people lived in Hungary, for-- I think before the Hungarians, really. From 1,900 years-- 1,900 years, you know, that the Jews lived in Hungary, you know? And at least there was-- you know, when the Hungarians established, which was 1,000 years back, there was also Jews came with them. You understand?

speaker: interviewee
text: But of course, the anti-Semitism, you know, was there. You know, you felt it all the time, of course. You know, constantly accused, being accused of killing Christ, and so on and so forth, and being different. Of course, you know, a lot of, a lot of people-- you know, like for instance, I was telling you, you know, like with Romanians--
